sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=30, col=62).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 144, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE_on_block': 'Toggles individual block between gray/white and green (N) (VERIFIED steps 3-14)', 'pattern_completion': 'Completing diamond pattern resets grid to all-gray and advances cursor on bar (VERIFIED step 15)', 'diamond_pattern': 'Click top(50,30), left(55,25), right(55,35), bottom(60,30) to complete pattern and advance cursor by 1'}
- Object taxonomy: {'grid_blocks': '3x3 clickable grid at rows 49-61, cols 24-36', 'purple_dots': '4 purple dots in diamond pattern - target pattern reference', 'bar': 'Horizontal bar with sky blue cursor that advances on pattern completion', 'right_strip': '2-col wide strip at cols 62-63, fills green from bottom as patterns completed'}
- HUD map: {'right_strip': 'Right strip (cols 62-63) fills with green 1 row per pattern completed. Currently 8 green rows (56-63), 56 white rows (0-55)'}
- Win pattern: {'level_1': 'Unknown - diamond patterns advance cursor and fill right strip but level does not complete. Need to find completion trigger.'}
- Level log: {'level_1': '15 patterns completed (60 clicks). Cursor cycles on bar. Right strip has 8 green rows. Level NOT completing. Way over action budget (134 actions vs ~36 target).'}
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 143 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 4.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 47 actions returned the board to a state already seen 47 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 47 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 8 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the right strip didn't change anything (game still over). The state shows:
- Right strip: fully green (rows 0-63)
- Cursor: at start (13-16) and at end (41-42)
- White blocks: the 4 diamond positions are white (I clicked them before game over)
The game is in a "game over" state. I need to figure out how to restart. Let me think about what I know:
1. The right strip is fully green now
2. The cursor is at both start and end positions
3. The grid has white blocks at diamond positions
Wait - looking at the current image, the right strip is FULLY GREEN. And the cursor is at the END of the bar (cols 41-42). This looks like the level might actually be complete now - the right strip is fully filled!
But the system says "game_over: true" and "level_completed: false". This is confusing.
Actually, wait - let me re-read the earlier output. When I clicked non-diamond blocks, it triggered game over AND the right strip filled completely. So game over = right strip full = game ends (but not as a "win").
The key question is: how do I WIN instead of losing? The answer must be to fill the right strip by completing diamond patterns, not by triggering game over.
But I've been completing diamond patterns and the right strip only filled 8 rows after 15 patterns. At that rate, it would take forever to fill all 64 rows.
Unless... the rate changes. Let me think about this differently:
- After 15 patterns: 8 green rows
- After game over (clicking wrong blocks): 64 green rows
What if the right strip fills based on how many TIMES the cursor completes a full cycle? Each cycle might add more rows.
Or what if there's a different mechanic I'm missing. Let me look at the bar more carefully:
- The bar has a start position (large cursor, 4x4)
- The bar has segments
- The cursor moves along the bar
What if I need to complete the pattern when the cursor is at a SPECIFIC position on the bar? Like, the cursor position matters for what happens next?
Actually, I just realized something. Let me look at the cursor positions again:
- Before game over: cursor at cols 13-16 (start) and col 32 (small)
- After game over: cursor at cols 13-16 (start) and cols 41-42 (end)
The cursor moved from col 32 to cols 41-42. This might be significant.
Wait, I think I need to reconsider the whole game. Let me look at the initial state again. The game started with:
- A bar with a cursor at the start
- A 3x3 grid of gray blocks
- A purple diamond pattern reference
- A white right strip
The goal might be to:
1. Complete diamond patterns to advance the cursor
2. When the cursor reaches the END, something special happens
3. The level completes when the right strip is fully green
But the rate of filling is too slow. Unless...
What if I need to complete patterns FASTER? Like, what if there's a time limit and I need to complete patterns before time runs out?
Or what if the game has a different mechanic where I need to click blocks in a specific ORDER or at a specific TIME?
Actually, let me reconsider. The game is now in "game over" state. I need to restart. Let me try clicking on the large cursor at the start position (cols 13-16) to see if that resets the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over state - need to restart. Try clicking the large cursor at start position.
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 15}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE_on_block": "Toggles block color. ONLY diamond blocks valid: (50,30),(55,25),(55,35),(60,30). Clicking others = GAME OVER.", "pattern_completion": "Completing diamond pattern resets grid and advances cursor", "diamond_pattern": "Click 4 diamond blocks to complete pattern"}, "object_taxonomy": {"grid_blocks": "3x3 grid, only 4 diamond positions clickable", "purple_dots": "Diamond pattern reference", "bar": "Progress bar with cursor", "right_strip": "Progress indicator, fills green"}, "hud_map": {"right_strip": "Fully green after game over"}, "win_pattern": {"level_1": "Fill right strip by completing diamond patterns without triggering game over"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "GAME OVER from clicking non-diamond blocks. Need to restart and find correct completion path."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Game over. Right strip fully green. Cursor at start and end.", "goal_model": "Restart level, then complete patterns correctly", "recent_findings": ["Clicking non-diamond blocks = game over", "Right strip fills completely on game over"], "open_questions": ["How to restart after game over?"], "current_plan": "Click start cursor to try restart", "strategy_log": ["Must only click diamond blocks"], "failed_probes": ["Clicking all 9 blocks - game over", "Clicking right strip - no change"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
